events.text: The patient is a 45-year-old woman named Maria Elena Reyes. She is of Hispanic/Latina ethnicity, born in Mexico, residing in the southwestern United States. She works as a high school teacher. She is premenopausal, with no prior personal history of breast or ovarian cancer. Family history is notable for a maternal aunt diagnosed with breast cancer at age 52. She has no known BRCA mutation history at time of diagnosis. She is a never-smoker, drinks alcohol occasionally, and has a BMI of 26.4. She has two children.

At age 45 years, the patient had a diagnosis of Stage IIB (AJCC 8th edition, cT2N1M0) INVASIVE DUCTAL CARCINOMA, NOS (MALIGNANT, PRIMARY) of the LEFT BREAST, upper outer quadrant (ICD-O site code C50.4). Histology code: 8500/3 (Infiltrating duct carcinoma, NOS). Grade: Grade 3 (Nottingham score 9/9; tubule formation 3, nuclear pleomorphism 3, mitotic rate 3). Biomarkers: Estrogen receptor (ER): Negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining); Progesterone receptor (PR): Negative (Allred score 0/8, 0% staining); HER2 IHC: 1+ (negative); HER2 FISH: not amplified (HER2/CEP17 ratio 1.2); Ki-67: 85% (high proliferative index). Clinical presentation: palpable mass in left upper outer breast, approximately 3.2 cm by clinical exam. Axillary lymph node: one palpable, clinically suspicious left axillary node. Confirmation: POSITIVE HISTOLOGY via core needle biopsy. Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) subtype confirmed. No evidence of distant metastasis on staging workup.

At age 45 years, the patient had a diagnostic mammogram and ultrasound of bilateral breasts and left axilla. Mammography showed a 3.4 cm irregular, spiculated mass in the left upper outer quadrant with associated architectural distortion. No suspicious microcalcifications. Ultrasound confirmed a 3.2 x 2.8 x 2.5 cm hypoechoic, irregular mass with posterior acoustic shadowing in the left breast at 10 o'clock position, 4 cm from the nipple. Left axillary ultrasound showed one morphologically abnormal lymph node at level I with cortical thickening to 8 mm and loss of fatty hilum, suspicious for nodal metastasis. Right breast and axilla: no suspicious findings. BI-RADS category 5 (highly suggestive of malignancy). Cancer status: cancer present (primary left breast mass and suspicious left axillary lymph node).

At age 45 years, the patient had a pathology result of type CORE NEEDLE BIOPSY (left breast mass, 10 o'clock position). Gross description: multiple tan-white core biopsy fragments, aggregate 1.4 cm. Microscopic description: invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3 (Nottingham grade 9/9). Tumor cells arranged in solid nests and sheets with minimal tubule formation. High nuclear grade with prominent nucleoli and frequent mitotic figures (>20 per 10 HPF). Stromal lymphocytic infiltrate (TIL score approximately 40%). No ductal carcinoma in situ identified in cores. Lymphovascular invasion: present. IHC panel: ER: negative (0%, Allred 0); PR: negative (0%, Allred 0); HER2: 1+ by IHC; HER2 FISH: not amplified. Ki-67: 85%. PD-L1 (22C3 antibody): Combined Positive Score (CPS) = 18 (positive; threshold >/=10 for pembrolizumab eligibility). Concurrent fine needle aspiration of suspicious left axillary lymph node: positive for malignant cells consistent with metastatic carcinoma.

At age 45 years, the patient had a CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast for staging purposes. CT chest: no pulmonary nodules, no pleural effusion, no mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy. Left axillary lymph node: 1.8 cm, morphologically abnormal, consistent with known metastasis. No right axillary adenopathy. CT abdomen: liver, spleen, pancreas, adrenal glands, and kidneys unremarkable. No hepatic lesions suspicious for metastasis. No retroperitoneal adenopathy. CT pelvis: no suspicious pelvic lymphadenopathy or osseous lesions. Bone windows: no lytic or blastic lesions. Impression: Stage IIB disease, no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Cancer status: cancer present, locoregional disease only (primary left breast and left axillary node).

At age 45 years, the patient had an MRI of bilateral breasts with and without contrast (pre-treatment baseline). Left breast: 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm irregular, heterogeneously enhancing mass at 10 o'clock, 4 cm from the nipple. Rapid initial enhancement with washout kinetics. No skin thickening or chest wall involvement. No satellite nodules or additional suspicious enhancing foci. No nipple involvement. Left axilla: three level I lymph nodes with abnormal morphology, largest 1.9 cm (one more than identified by ultrasound). Right breast: no suspicious enhancement, no mass. Contralateral axilla: normal. MRI staging: cT2N1M0. Impression: locally advanced left breast primary with left axillary nodal involvement, no evidence of additional occult malignancy. Cancer status: cancer present.

At age 45 years, the patient had next-generation sequencing performed for triple-negative breast cancer based on a specimen obtained from the left breast core needle biopsy (primary tumor). Comprehensive genomic profiling (Foundation Medicine FoundationOne CDx) revealed the following alterations: ACTIONABLE/CLINICALLY RELEVANT: TP53 p.R248W mutation (pathogenic, VAF 48%, co-mutation common in TNBC); PIK3CA wildtype; BRCA1 wildtype (tumor); BRCA2 wildtype (tumor); PTEN loss (homozygous deletion, copy number 0); AKT1 wildtype. ADDITIONAL ALTERATIONS: MYC amplification (copy number 18, high-level amplification); RB1 p.R579* (nonsense mutation, VAF 42%, loss of function); CDKN2A homozygous deletion; CDK6 amplification (copy number 7); CCND1 wildtype; MLL3 (KMT2C) p.E1285* (VAF 31%); ARID1A p.Q1378* (VAF 28%); SPEN p.Q1690fs (frameshift, VAF 22%); FAT1 p.R2428H (VAF 19%); NF1 p.E1803* (VAF 18%); NOTCH1 p.P2461fs (frameshift, VAF 15%). FUSIONS/REARRANGEMENTS: none detected. COPY NUMBER ALTERATIONS: MYC gain/amplification (chr 8q24), CDK6 gain (chr 7q21), PTEN loss (chr 10q23), CDKN2A loss (chr 9p21), RB1 loss (chr 13q14). TMB: 8 mutations/Mb (intermediate). MSI: microsatellite stable (MSS). PD-L1 CPS 18 (as per IHC, consistent with immunotherapy eligibility). Germline testing (separate panel): BRCA1/2 wildtype; PALB2 wildtype; CHEK2 wildtype; ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance). HRD score (genomic instability): 42 (borderline, below threshold of 42.5 for olaparib eligibility per gBRCA-negative criteria). Note: Given TP53 mutation, MYC amplification, RB1 loss, and CDKN2A deletion, this tumor has a genomic profile consistent with high-grade, highly aggressive TNBC.

At age 45 years, the patient had a multidisciplinary tumor board (MDT) assessment and new patient oncology consultation. Case reviewed by breast medical oncology, surgical oncology, radiation oncology, and pathology. Diagnosis: Stage IIB (cT2N1M0) triple-negative breast cancer, grade 3, left breast. PD-L1 CPS 18 (positive). KI-67 85%. Patient is premenopausal, ECOG performance status 0. No comorbidities. Plan: neoadjuvant systemic therapy with intent to downstage and assess pathologic response, then surgery. Recommended regimen: pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks plus dose-dense AC (doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 q2 weeks x 4 cycles) followed by weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 x 12 doses with pembrolizumab q3 weeks, based on KEYNOTE-522 trial data. Pegfilgrastim support planned. Patient counseled on goals of therapy, expected toxicities (nausea, fatigue, alopecia, neuropathy, immune-related adverse events), fertility preservation (patient declined oocyte cryopreservation after counseling), and surgical options post-neoadjuvant therapy. Patient consented and is eager to proceed. Restaging MRI planned after completion of neoadjuvant therapy. Cancer present, initiating treatment.

At age 45 years, the patient received neoadjuvant-intent therapy: Cycle 1 of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on day 1 (q3 week schedule) combined with dose-dense doxorubicin 60 mg/m2 IV and cyclophosphamide 600 mg/m2 IV (AC chemotherapy, q2 week schedule). Pegfilgrastim 6 mg subcutaneous injection administered day 2. Pre-medications: ondansetron, dexamethasone, lorazepam. This initiated the AC phase (4 planned cycles, q2 weeks) with pembrolizumab running concurrently on a q3-week cycle. Day 1 of neoadjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 2 weeks into treatment), the patient reported Grade 2 nausea and Grade 1 fatigue following Cycle 1 of AC. She also developed Grade 1 alopecia, which progressed to Grade 2 (complete scalp hair loss) by week 3. Anti-emetic regimen optimized: added prochlorperazine as breakthrough agent and olanzapine 5 mg nightly x 3 days per cycle. No febrile neutropenia. CBC nadir: ANC 1.2 x 10^9/L (Grade 2 neutropenia), recovered appropriately by day 14. No dose modifications required. No immune-related adverse events noted at this time.

At age 45 years, the patient received Cycles 2, 3, and 4 of dose-dense AC (doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide) q2 weeks with pegfilgrastim support, and pembrolizumab 200 mg IV on the schedule aligned with cycles 1 and 3 of AC (i.e., pembrolizumab doses 1, 2 administered during the AC phase). All four AC cycles administered at full dose. Cycle 4 AC completed approximately 8 weeks from initiation. Tolerance was acceptable with optimized antiemetics. No febrile neutropenia, no Grade 3/4 toxicity during AC phase. Patient transitioned to paclitaxel phase.

At age 45 years (during the paclitaxel phase, approximately week 14 of neoadjuvant therapy), the patient developed Grade 2 peripheral sensory neuropathy, bilateral hands and feet, described as tingling and mild numbness. Weekly paclitaxel was dose-reduced by 20% (from 80 mg/m2 to 64 mg/m2) for weeks 9-12 of paclitaxel per institutional protocol. Additionally, the patient developed Grade 2 immune-related arthralgia/arthritis affecting bilateral knee and wrist joints, attributed to pembrolizumab. Rheumatology consultation obtained. Prednisone 20 mg daily initiated with plan to taper over 4 weeks. Pembrolizumab was held for one cycle. After clinical improvement to Grade 1 with steroids, pembrolizumab was resumed at full dose. No colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, or endocrinopathy identified.

At age 45 years, the patient completed the paclitaxel phase: weekly paclitaxel 80 mg/m2 (with dose reduction to 64 mg/m2 for weeks 9-12 as noted) x 12 total doses administered over approximately 12 weeks, with pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks (4 additional doses during this phase, for a total of 6 pembrolizumab doses during neoadjuvant therapy). Full course of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/AC/paclitaxel completed. Total neoadjuvant therapy duration: approximately 20 weeks. Patient referred back to surgical oncology for definitive surgery.

At age 45 years (post-neoadjuvant, pre-surgery), the patient had an MRI of bilateral breasts with and without contrast for restaging. Left breast: residual irregular enhancing mass measuring 1.4 x 1.1 x 0.9 cm at 10 o'clock (previously 3.5 x 2.9 x 2.7 cm), representing a partial radiographic response (approximately 60% size reduction). Residual heterogeneous enhancement pattern, no washout kinetics identified in residual area (pattern changed from pre-treatment). Left axilla: previously abnormal level I lymph nodes now appear markedly improved; the largest node measures 0.7 cm with partial fatty hilum restoration, though one node remains borderline. No new suspicious lesions in either breast. No chest wall or skin involvement. Impression: partial radiographic response to neoadjuvant therapy, residual left breast mass with uncertain but possible residual nodal disease. Cancer status: cancer likely still present (residual), responding to therapy (partial response by imaging).

At age 45 years, the patient underwent left breast surgery and axillary staging. Procedure: left partial mastectomy (lumpectomy/breast-conserving surgery) with sentinel lymph node biopsy and left axillary lymph node dissection (ALND). The surgical oncologist performed wire-localized excision of the residual left breast mass with tumor bed clip localization. Sentinel lymph node biopsy identified 3 sentinel nodes; intraoperative frozen section of one sentinel node showed metastatic carcinoma, prompting completion ALND. Surgical findings: firm, ill-defined 1.5 cm residual tumor bed in left upper outer quadrant. Total lymph nodes removed: 16 (3 sentinel, 13 additional axillary). Estimated blood loss: 80 mL. No complications. Specimen sent to pathology. Closure performed in standard fashion. Post-operative recovery uneventful.

At age 45 years, the patient had a pathology result of type SURGICAL RESECTION (left partial mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection). Gross description: left breast partial mastectomy specimen, 7.5 x 6.0 x 4.2 cm. Tumor bed identified with clip. Residual tumor: 1.2 cm focus of residual invasive ductal carcinoma, grade 3, at the excision site. Margins: all surgical margins negative (closest margin 4 mm posterior). Residual DCIS: not identified. Lymphovascular invasion: present in residual tumor. Tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes in residual tumor: 15% (reduced from 40% pre-treatment). Treatment effect: moderate (Miller-Payne grade 3, approximately 50-75% reduction in tumor cellularity; RCB class II). Axillary lymph nodes: 3 of 16 lymph nodes positive for metastatic carcinoma (3/16); largest metastatic deposit 0.8 cm; no extranodal extension. ypT1cN1M0, Stage IIA (post-neoadjuvant). Pathologic assessment: NON-pCR (residual invasive disease present). Residual Cancer Burden (RCB) score: 2.8 (RCB-II, intermediate residual disease). IHC on residual tumor: ER negative, PR negative, HER2 IHC 1+ (negative), consistent with persistent TNBC phenotype.

At age 45 years (post-surgical, approximately 6 weeks after surgery), the patient had a post-operative oncology visit. Wound healing satisfactory. Pathology reviewed: ypT1cN1M0, RCB-II, non-pCR. Discussion of adjuvant therapy options. Given residual disease after neoadjuvant pembrolizumab/chemotherapy, plan for: (1) adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 twice daily x 14 days every 21 days x 8 cycles (per CREATE-X trial), and (2) continuation of pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks to complete 1 year total (approximately 9 additional doses to reach 17 total pembrolizumab doses, consistent with KEYNOTE-522 protocol). Radiation oncology referral placed for adjuvant radiation (post-mastectomy/post-lumpectomy radiation to left breast and nodal basins given 3+ positive nodes). Patient tolerated surgical recovery well. ECOG PS 1. She has begun to experience lingering Grade 1 neuropathy from paclitaxel. Alopecia improving (early regrowth noted). Cancer status: post-surgery, residual disease confirmed, starting adjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 8 weeks post-surgery), the patient underwent adjuvant radiation therapy to the left breast and regional lymphatics. Radiation technique: intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) to left breast and left chest wall (tumor bed boost included). Fields: whole breast, supraclavicular fossa, axillary levels I-III, internal mammary chain. Dose: whole breast 50 Gy in 25 fractions (2 Gy/fraction, 5 days/week over 5 weeks), followed by tumor bed boost 10 Gy in 5 fractions. Regional nodal irradiation: supraclavicular 50 Gy in 25 fractions, axillary 46 Gy in 23 fractions. Total RT duration: approximately 6 weeks. Radiation was sequenced to begin concurrently with capecitabine initiation (capecitabine held during radiation per institutional protocol to minimize skin toxicity, then resumed). Pembrolizumab continued on schedule during radiation.

At age 45 years (during radiation therapy, approximately week 4 of RT), the patient developed Grade 2 radiation dermatitis of the left breast and axilla with moist desquamation in the axillary fold. Managed with silver sulfadiazine cream, moisture-wicking dressings, and treatment break of 3 days. Completed radiation without further interruption. She also developed Grade 1 radiation pneumonitis (mild cough, no dyspnea, no infiltrate on CXR) approximately 6 weeks after completion of radiation, managed conservatively with low-dose prednisone 10 mg daily x 3 weeks with taper. No significant fatigue beyond baseline. Pembrolizumab was continued throughout radiation as pneumonitis was mild and attributed to radiation rather than immune-related etiology.

At age 45 years, the patient initiated adjuvant capecitabine 1000 mg/m2 orally twice daily (days 1-14, every 21 days), beginning approximately 10 weeks post-surgery (after completion of radiation). Target: 8 cycles total. Concurrent pembrolizumab 200 mg IV q3 weeks continued (adjuvant maintenance phase, to complete approximately 1 year total from initiation of pembrolizumab). Uridine diphosphate glucuronosyltransferase (DPYD) genotyping performed prior to capecitabine: no pathogenic variant identified (DPYD wildtype). Patient counseled on hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, mucositis, and capecitabine-drug interactions.

At age 45 years (Cycle 3 of adjuvant capecitabine, approximately 3 months into adjuvant therapy), the patient developed Grade 2 hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia): bilateral palmar and plantar erythema, swelling, and pain interfering with daily activities. Capecitabine dose reduced to 750 mg/m2 twice daily (25% dose reduction). Initiated urea-based emollient cream (Eucerin) to hands and feet twice daily, vitamin B6 supplementation, and avoidance of friction/heat. Grade 2 diarrhea (4-6 loose stools per day above baseline) also noted in Cycle 3, managed with loperamide and oral hydration; resolved within 5 days. No Grade 3/4 toxicities. No further pembrolizumab immune-related adverse events during this phase.

At age 45 years (approximately 6 months post-surgery, during adjuvant therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast. CT chest: no new pulmonary nodules. Post-radiation changes in left lung base (minor ground-glass opacity, consistent with prior radiation pneumonitis, resolving). No pleural effusion. Left axillary surgical bed: post-surgical changes, no new adenopathy. CT abdomen and pelvis: no hepatic lesions, no peritoneal nodules, no retroperitoneal or pelvic adenopathy. No ascites. Skeletal survey (bone windows): no new lytic or blastic lesions. Impression: no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-treatment changes only. Cancer status: no cancer detected on imaging, consistent with no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 45 years (approximately 7 months post-surgery), the patient had a routine oncology visit during adjuvant therapy. She is currently on Cycle 5 of capecitabine (dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID) and has received 14 of 17 planned pembrolizumab doses total. Tolerating therapy reasonably well. Hand-foot syndrome improved to Grade 1 on dose-reduced capecitabine. Peripheral neuropathy Grade 1, stable. No new immune-related adverse events. Thyroid function tests: TSH 7.8 mIU/L (mildly elevated, new finding); free T4 low normal. Consistent with immune-mediated hypothyroidism (pembrolizumab-related). Endocrinology consultation placed. Levothyroxine 50 mcg daily initiated. Repeat TSH planned in 6 weeks. ECOG PS 1. Surveillance imaging (CT) reviewed - no evidence of disease. Cancer status: no evidence of disease, on adjuvant treatment.

At age 45 years (approximately 8 months post-surgery), the patient was confirmed to have Grade 1-2 immune-mediated hypothyroidism secondary to pembrolizumab. TSH stabilized at 4.2 mIU/L on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily (dose uptitrated from 50 mcg). Per KEYNOTE-522 protocol guidance, hypothyroidism managed with hormone replacement; pembrolizumab continued without interruption as endocrinopathy is an expected manageable immune-related adverse event not requiring drug discontinuation. Patient asymptomatic from hypothyroidism. No other immune-related adverse events identified. Pembrolizumab maintenance continued.

At age 46 years, the patient completed all 8 cycles of adjuvant capecitabine (Cycle 8 completed) and received the final (17th) dose of pembrolizumab to complete approximately 1 year of total pembrolizumab therapy (counting neoadjuvant + adjuvant doses). Capecitabine was administered at reduced dose (750 mg/m2 BID) for cycles 3-8 due to hand-foot syndrome. All planned adjuvant therapy completed. Patient transitioned to active surveillance. Levothyroxine for immune-mediated hypothyroidism continued indefinitely.

At age 46 years (approximately 12 months post-surgery, end of adjuvant therapy), the patient had a surveillance CT scan of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast and bilateral breast MRI. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no evidence of distant metastatic disease. Post-treatment changes in left lung stable. No new lymphadenopathy, no hepatic lesions, no osseous lesions. Breast MRI: left breast surgical site with expected post-operative and post-radiation changes; no suspicious new enhancement. Right breast: no suspicious enhancement. Bilateral axillae: no pathologic adenopathy. Impression: no evidence of local recurrence or distant metastasis. Cancer status: no cancer detected, no evidence of disease (NED).

At age 46 years (12-month post-surgery surveillance visit), the patient had a routine oncology follow-up. She has completed all planned neoadjuvant and adjuvant therapies. She is now on active surveillance. Performance status: ECOG 0 (returned to full activity, teaching full-time). Peripheral neuropathy: resolved to Grade 0 (complete resolution). Hand-foot syndrome: resolved. Alopecia: complete hair regrowth. Hypothyroidism: stable on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH within normal limits. She reports improved energy and quality of life. Psychosocial: patient engaged with peer support group for young women with breast cancer; seeing a therapist for cancer-related anxiety and body image concerns; no active depression. Reproductive discussion: patient interested in future pregnancy considerations; referred to reproductive endocrinology for counseling on ovarian function and pregnancy safety post-TNBC. Menstrual cycles resumed post-chemotherapy (chemotherapy-associated amenorrhea resolved at 9 months). Plan: clinical exam and labs every 3-4 months x 2 years, then every 6 months. Annual bilateral breast MRI and mammography. Continue levothyroxine. Genetic counseling referral for updated BRCA testing with expanded panel given new family history information (maternal cousin recently diagnosed with ovarian cancer). Cancer status: no evidence of disease.

At age 46 years (18-month post-surgery surveillance), the patient had a surveillance bilateral breast MRI. Left breast: stable post-operative/post-radiation changes at surgical site. No new suspicious enhancement. No mass or architectural distortion. Right breast: no suspicious enhancement. Bilateral axillae: no pathologic adenopathy. Compared to prior MRI (12 months post-surgery): no interval change. Impression: no evidence of local recurrence. Cancer status: no cancer detected.

At age 46 years (18-month post-surgery surveillance), the patient had a routine oncology visit. She remains without evidence of disease. ECOG PS 0. No new symptoms. Continues levothyroxine 75 mcg daily; TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal). Peripheral neuropathy: Grade 0 (fully resolved). Expanded germline panel resulted: BRCA1 wildtype, BRCA2 wildtype, PALB2 wildtype, RAD51C wildtype, RAD51D wildtype, BRIP1 wildtype, ATM p.V2424G (variant of uncertain significance, classification unchanged). Genetic counselor discussed implications with patient; no change in screening or management recommended based on VUS. Patient continues annual mammography and MRI surveillance per NCCN high-risk guidelines. She reports good quality of life. She has returned to all pre-diagnosis activities. Discussion of ongoing cardiovascular surveillance given prior anthracycline exposure: baseline echocardiogram performed (LVEF 62%, normal). Plan: continue surveillance per schedule. Cancer status: no evidence of disease at 18 months post-surgery.

scenario_blurb: Triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) stage IIB in a 45-year-old woman, diagnosed after palpable mass and core needle biopsy showing grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma, ER/PR/HER2 negative, Ki-67 85%. Treated with neoadjuvant pembrolizumab plus dose-dense AC followed by paclitaxel, then surgery. Mixed pathologic response with residual disease, followed by adjuvant capecitabine and pembrolizumab maintenance.
